name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
كٓهيعٓصٓ (١)
```

```english
Kaf, Ha, Ya, ‘Ayn, Suad. (1)
```

```arabic
ذِكْرُ رَحْمَتِ رَبِّكَ عَبْدَهُۥ زَكَرِيَّآ (٢)
```

```english
[This is] an account of your Lord’s mercy on His servant, Zechariah, (2)
```

```arabic
إِذْ نَادَىٰ رَبَّهُۥ نِدَآءً خَفِيًّا (٣)
```

```english
when he called out to his Lord with a secret cry. (3)
```

```arabic
قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّى وَهَنَ ٱلْعَظْمُ مِنِّى وَٱشْتَعَلَ ٱلرَّأْسُ شَيْبًا وَلَمْ أَكُنۢ بِدُعَآئِكَ رَبِّ شَقِيًّا (٤)
```

```english
He said, ‘My Lord! Indeed my bones have become feeble, and my head has turned white with age, yet never have I, my Lord, been disappointed in supplicating You! (4)
```

```arabic
وَإِنِّى خِفْتُ ٱلْمَوَٰلِىَ مِن وَرَآءِى وَكَانَتِ ٱمْرَأَتِى عَاقِرًا فَهَبْ لِى مِن لَّدُنكَ وَلِيًّا (٥)
```

```english
Indeed I fear my kinsmen, after me, and my wife is barren. So grant me from Yourself an heir (5)
```

```arabic
يَرِثُنِى وَيَرِثُ مِنْ ءَالِ يَعْقُوبَ ۖ وَٱجْعَلْهُ رَبِّ رَضِيًّا (٦)
```

```english
who may inherit from me and inherit from the House of Jacob, and make him, my Lord, pleasing [to You]!’ (6)
```

```arabic
يَـٰزَكَرِيَّآ إِنَّا نُبَشِّرُكَ بِغُلَـٰمٍ ٱسْمُهُۥ يَحْيَىٰ لَمْ نَجْعَل لَّهُۥ مِن قَبْلُ سَمِيًّا (٧)
```

```english
‘O Zechariah! Indeed We give you the good news of a son, whose name is ‘‘John.’’ Never before have We made anyone his namesake.’ (7)
```

```arabic
قَالَ رَبِّ أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لِى غُلَـٰمٌ وَكَانَتِ ٱمْرَأَتِى عَاقِرًا وَقَدْ بَلَغْتُ مِنَ ٱلْكِبَرِ عِتِيًّا (٨)
```

```english
He said, ‘My Lord! How shall I have a son, when my wife is barren, and I am already advanced in age?’ (8)
```

```arabic
قَالَ كَذَٰلِكَ قَالَ رَبُّكَ هُوَ عَلَىَّ هَيِّنٌ وَقَدْ خَلَقْتُكَ مِن قَبْلُ وَلَمْ تَكُ شَيْـًٔا (٩)
```

```english
He said, ‘So shall it be. Your Lord has said, ‘‘It is simple for Me.’’ Certainly I created you before when you were nothing.’ (9)
```

```arabic
قَالَ رَبِّ ٱجْعَل لِّىٓ ءَايَةً ۚ قَالَ ءَايَتُكَ أَلَّا تُكَلِّمَ ٱلنَّاسَ ثَلَـٰثَ لَيَالٍ سَوِيًّا (١٠)
```

```english
He said, ‘My Lord! Appoint a sign for me.’ He said, ‘Your sign is that you will not speak to the people for three complete nights.’ (10)
```

```arabic
فَخَرَجَ عَلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ مِنَ ٱلْمِحْرَابِ فَأَوْحَىٰٓ إِلَيْهِمْ أَن سَبِّحُوا۟ بُكْرَةً وَعَشِيًّا (١١)
```

```english
So he emerged before his people from the Temple, and signalled to them that they should glorify [Allah] morning and evening. (11)
```

```arabic
يَـٰيَحْيَىٰ خُذِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِقُوَّةٍ ۖ وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ ٱلْحُكْمَ صَبِيًّا (١٢)
```

```english
‘O John!’ [We said,] ‘Hold on with power to the Book!’ And We gave him judgement while still a child, (12)
```

```arabic
وَحَنَانًا مِّن لَّدُنَّا وَزَكَوٰةً ۖ وَكَانَ تَقِيًّا (١٣)
```

```english
and a compassion and purity from Us. He was Godwary (13)
```

```arabic
وَبَرًّۢا بِوَٰلِدَيْهِ وَلَمْ يَكُن جَبَّارًا عَصِيًّا (١٤)
```

```english
and good to his parents, and was not self-willed or disobedient. (14)
```

```arabic
وَسَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْهِ يَوْمَ وُلِدَ وَيَوْمَ يَمُوتُ وَيَوْمَ يُبْعَثُ حَيًّا (١٥)
```

```english
Peace be to him, the day he was born, and the day he dies, and the day he is raised alive! (15)
```

## (Commentary)

The purpose of the Surah, as indicated by the saying of the Exalted at its conclusion: **فَإِنَّمَا يَسَّرْنَاهُ بِلِسَانِكَ لِتُبَشِّرَ بِهِ الْمُتَّقِينَ وَتُنذِرَ بِهِ قَوْمًا لُّدًّا** — "Indeed We have made it simple in your language so that you may give good news thereby to the God-conscious (المتقين) — *those who possess godwariness and a protective shield against disobedience to Allah* — and warn with it a contentious people" (19:97) — is to provide glad tidings and warnings. However, it presents this discourse in a wondrous manner. It first points to the story of Zechariah and John, the story of Mary and Jesus, the story of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, the story of Moses and Aaron, the story of Ishmael, and the story of Idris, along with the specific blessings of guardianship (الولاية) — *the divine proximity and authority granted by Allah* — such as prophethood (النبوة), truthfulness (الصدق), and sincerity (الإخلاص) that He bestowed upon them. Then, it mentions that those upon whom He bestowed these blessings were known for their submission (الخضوع) and humility (الخشوع) toward their Lord. However, their successors turned away from that, neglected the matter of turning toward their Lord, and followed their lusts; thus, they will encounter perdition (الغيا) and stray from right guidance (الرشد), unless one of them repents and returns to his Lord, for he shall then join the people of blessing.

Then, the Surah mentions some of the lapses of the people of perdition and their arbitrary assertions, such as their denial of the Resurrection (المعاد), their saying: **اتَّخَذَ اللهُ وَلَداً** — "Allah has taken a son," their worship of idols, and the exemplary punishment (النكال) and torment that will afflict them because of that.

Thus, the exposition in the Surah most closely resembles the demonstration of a claim by citing examples. It is as if it were said: "So-and-so, so-and-so, and so-and-so, who were the people of right guidance and divine gift, their way was to detach themselves from the lusts of the soul and turn toward their Lord; their path was submission and humility when they were reminded of the signs of their Lord. This is the human path to right guidance and blessing. However, their successors abandoned this path by turning away from righteous deeds (صالح العمل) and inclining toward blameworthy lusts. This leads them only to perdition — the opposite of right guidance — and establishes them only upon falsehood, such as denying the return to Allah, affirming partners for Allah, and blocking the path of the call (الدعوة). It guides them to nothing but exemplary punishment and torment."

The Surah, as you see, opens by mentioning examples and follows them by extracting the universal (الكلي) meaning intended for exposition, which is His saying: **أُولَئِكَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِم** — "They are the ones whom Allah has blessed..." (19:58). Thus, the Surah divides people into three groups: those whom Allah has blessed among the prophets and the people of election (الاجتباء) and guidance (الهدى); the people of perdition; and those who repent, believe, and perform righteous deeds, who are joined with the people of blessing and right guidance. It then mentions the reward of the repentant seekers of guidance and the punishment of the erring ones, who are the companions and allies of the devils.

The Surah is Meccan (مكية) without doubt; the contents of its verses indicate this, and the consensus of the commentators has been narrated to that effect.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كهيعص** — It has already been mentioned in the commentary (تفسير) of the beginning of Surah al-A'raf that the Quranic Surahs prefaced with disjointed letters (الحروف المقطعة) are not devoid of a connection between their contents and those letters. Shared letters reveal shared contents.

This is supported by the affinity and homogeneity we find between this Surah and Surah Sad in the narration of the stories of the prophets. A comprehensive discussion will reach you, Allah willing, regarding the links between the disjointed letters and the contents of the Surahs they preface, as well as the connection between Surahs that share some of these letters, such as this Surah and Surah Ya-Sin, which share the letter *Ya* (الياء), and this Surah and Surah al-Shura, which share the letter *'Ayn* (العين).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ذِكْرُ رَحْمَتِ رَبِّكَ عَبْدَهُ زَكَرِيَّا** — "[This is] an account of your Lord’s mercy on His servant Zechariah" (19:2). The apparent context is that "account" (ذِكْرُ) is the predicate (خبر) of a suppressed subject (مبتدء), and the verbal noun (المصدر) carries the meaning of the object (المفعول). The ultimate meaning according to the estimation is: "This is the account of your Lord's mentioned mercy." What is meant by mercy (الرحمة) is His response, the Glorified, to the prayer of Zechariah in the detail that He narrated, as evidenced by His subsequent saying: **إِذْ نادى رَبَّهُ** — "When he called out to his Lord."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِذْ نادى رَبَّهُ نِدَاءً خَفِيًّا** — "when he called out to his Lord with a secret cry" (19:3). The adverbial phrase (الظرف) relates to His saying: "your Lord's mercy." **Calling out (النداء)** and **calling (المناداة)** refer to raising the voice in supplication, which is the opposite of intimate discourse (المناجاة). This is not contradicted by its description as "secret" (خَفِيًّا), for it is possible to raise the voice in supplication in a place secluded from people where they cannot hear the prayer. This is suggested by His later saying: **فَخَرَجَ عَلى قَوْمِهِ مِنَ الْمِحْرابِ** — "So he emerged to his people from the sanctuary."

It has been said that the point of using the term "call" (النداء) is that he perceived himself as distant from Him, the Exalted, due to his sins and poor states, as is the condition of one who fears His punishment.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي وَهَنَ الْعَظْمُ مِنِّي** — "He said, ‘My Lord! Indeed my bones have become feeble...’" (19:4) until the end of the verse. This is a preamble to what he will ask for, which is His saying: **فَهَبْ لِي مِنْ لَدُنْكَ وَلِيًّا** — "so grant me from Yourself an heir."

He placed the word "My Lord" (رَبِّ) first to seek mercy at the opening of the prayer. The emphasis with "Indeed" (إِنِّي) indicates his realization of his need. **Feebleness (الوهن)** is weakness and the diminution of strength. He attributed it to the bone (العظم) because it is the pillar upon which the body relies for its movement and stillness. He did not say "my bones" (العظام مني) or "my bone" (عظمي) to indicate the genus (الجنس) and to provide detail after the general statement.

His saying: **وَاشْتَعَلَ الرَّأْسُ شَيْباً** — "and my head has flared up with hoariness" (19:4). **Flaring up (الاشتعال)** is the spreading of the flame and blaze of fire in a burning object. It is said in *al-Majma'*: "His saying: **وَاشْتَعَلَ الرَّأْسُ شَيْباً** is among the most beautiful metaphors (الاستعارات). The meaning is that grayness flared up in the head and spread, just as the radiance of fire spreads." What is meant by radiance here is the flame and blaze.

His saying: **وَلَمْ أَكُنْ بِدُعائِكَ رَبِّ شَقِيًّا** — "and never, my Lord, have I been disappointed in supplicating You" (19:4). **Wretchedness (الشقاوة)** is the opposite of felicity (السعادة). What is meant by it here is the deprivation of good, which is either a necessary consequence of wretchedness or wretchedness itself. His saying: "in supplicating You" (بِدُعائِكَ) relates to "disappointed/wretched" (الشقي), and the preposition *bi* (الباء) denotes causality (السببية) or carries the meaning of "in." The meaning is: "I have been felicitous because of my supplicating You; whenever I called upon You, You responded to me without making me wretched or depriving me." Or: "I have not been deprived or frustrated in my supplicating You; You have accustomed me to a response when I call upon You and acceptance when I ask of You." In any case, "supplication" (الدعاء) is a verbal noun added to the object.

It has been said that "in supplicating You" (بِدُعائِكَ) is a verbal noun added to the subject, meaning: "I have not been wretched, rebellious, or disobedient toward Your calling me to servitude and obedience; rather, I have been a worshiper of Yours, sincere in my obedience to You." The first meaning is more apparent.

In the repetition of His saying: "My Lord" (رَبِّ) and its placement between the noun of *kana* (اسم كان) and its predicate (خبرها) in His saying: **وَلَمْ أَكُنْ بِدُعائِكَ رَبِّ شَقِيًّا**, there is a level of eloquence (البلاغة) that cannot be measured. Its parallel is His saying: **وَاجْعَلْهُ رَبِّ رَضِيًّا** — "and make him, my Lord, pleasing [to You]."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنِّي خِفْتُ الْمَوالِيَ مِنْ وَرائِي وَكانَتِ امْرَأَتِي عاقِراً** — "Indeed I fear my kinsmen after me, and my wife is barren" (19:5). This is the completion of the preamble he provided for his prayer. What is meant by **kinsmen (الموالي)** are paternal uncles and cousins. It has been said: the distant relatives (الكلالة); it has been said: the agnates (العصبة); it has been said: the cousins only; and it has been said: the heirs. In any case, they are those other than direct offspring. The meaning is: "I fear the actions of the kinsmen after me," i.e., after my death. He (peace be upon him) feared dying without an offspring from his lineage who would inherit from him, which is an allusion (كناية) to his fear of dying without an heir.

His saying: **وَكانَتِ امْرَأَتِي عاقِراً** — "and my wife is barren." **The barren woman (العاقر)** is the woman who does not give birth. It is said: a barren woman (امرأة عاقر) who does not give birth, and a barren man (رجل عاقر) for whom no child is born. The expression "and my wife was (كانت)" indicates that his wife, despite being barren, had passed the age of childbearing at the time of the prayer.

The apparent lack of repetition of "Indeed" (إن) in His saying: "and my wife is..." indicates that the sentence is circumstantial (حالية), and the entire discourse from "Indeed I fear..." to "barren" is a single section intended to mean that the fact of my wife being barren necessitated my fear of the kinsmen after me and after my death. Thus, the entirety of what he prepared for the prayer boils down to two sections: one, that Allah, the Glorified, had accustomed him to a response throughout his life until he became old and decrepit; and the other, that he feared the kinsmen after his death due to his wife's barrenness. It is possible to view the discourse as three sections by taking his old age and his wife's barrenness as two independent sections.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَهَبْ لِي مِنْ لَدُنْكَ وَلِيًّا يَرِثُنِي وَيَرِثُ مِنْ آلِ يَعْقُوبَ وَاجْعَلْهُ رَبِّ رَضِيًّا** — "so grant me from Yourself an heir who may inherit from me and inherit from the House of Jacob, and make him, my Lord, pleasing [to You]" (19:5-6). This is the prayer. He qualified the divine gift he asked for with the phrase "from Yourself" (مِنْ لَدُنْكَ) because he had despaired of the ordinary causes (الأسباب العادية) available to him: himself, as he had become a decrepit old man with failing strength, and his wife, who had grown old and was previously barren.

The **guardian/heir (ولي)** of a person is the one who takes charge of his affairs. The guardian of the deceased is the one who carries out his affairs and succeeds him in what he left behind. The **House (آل)** of a man are his elite who return to his authority, such as his children, relatives, and companions. It has been said: its root is *ahl* (أهل). What is meant by Jacob, according to what has been said, is Jacob son of Isaac son of Abraham (peace be upon him). It has also been said he is Jacob son of Mathan, the brother of Imran son of Mathan, the father of Mary; Zechariah's wife was Mary's sister. According to this, the meaning of His saying: "who may inherit from me and inherit from the House of Jacob" would be: "who inherits from me and inherits from my wife, who is part of the House of Jacob." In that case, it is more likely that "from" (مِنْ) in His saying "from the House of Jacob" denotes partitivity (التبعيض), though it could also be initiatory (ابتدائية).

His saying: **وَاجْعَلْهُ رَبِّ رَضِيًّا** — "and make him, my Lord, pleasing [to You]." "Pleasing" (الرضي) carries the meaning of "one who is well-pleasing" (المرضي). The absolute use of "pleasing" necessitates its inclusion of both knowledge and action. Thus, what is meant is one who is well-pleasing in his belief and his action; i.e., "make him, my Lord, adorned with beneficial knowledge and righteous deeds."

Allah, the Glorified, narrated this story in Surah Al 'Imran—which is Medinan (مدنية) and later in revelation than the Meccan Surah Maryam—with His saying at the end of Mary's story: **فَتَقَبَّلَهَا رَبُّهَا بِقَبُولٍ حَسَنٍ وَأَنبَتَهَا نَبَاتًا حَسَنًا وَكَفَّلَهَا زَكَرِيَّا كُلَّمَا دَخَلَ عَلَيْهَا زَكَرِيَّا الْمِحْرَابَ وَجَدَ عِندَهَا رِزْقًا قَالَ يَا مَرْيَمُ أَنَّى لَكِ هَذَا قَالَتْ هُوَ مِنْ عِندِ اللهِ إِنَّ اللهَ يَرْزُقُ مَن يَشَاءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ هُنَالِكَ دَعَا زَكَرِيَّا رَبَّهُ قَالَ رَبِّ هَبْ لِي مِن لَّدُنْكَ ذُرِّيَّةً طَيِّبَةً إِنَّكَ سَمِيعُ الدُّعَاء** — "Thereupon her Lord accepted her with a gracious acceptance, and made her grow up in a worthy fashion, and He charged Zechariah with her care. Whenever Zechariah visited her in the sanctuary, he would find provisions with her. He said, ‘O Mary, from where does this come for you?’ She said, ‘It is from Allah. Indeed Allah provides for whomever He wishes without any reckoning.’ Thereat Zechariah supplicated his Lord. He said, ‘My Lord! Grant me a good offspring from Yourself! Indeed You are the Hearer of supplication’" (3:37-38).

The contemplative reader of the two verses will not doubt that what prompted Zechariah and drove him to his prayer was what he witnessed of Mary's state and her nobility before Allah, the Glorified, in her servitude and sincerity of action. He desired to have a successor who would possess a similar proximity and nobility to what he saw in Mary. Then he mentioned his own state of old age and exhausted strength, and his wife's state of advanced age and barrenness, while he had kinsmen whom he did not approve of. He found this to be the case while remembering what his Lord had accustomed him to regarding the response to prayer and the sufficiency in every important matter; so he fled to his Lord in prayer, seeking the gift of a good offspring.

Thus, his saying in Surah Al 'Imran: **رَبِّ هَبْ لِي مِن لَّدُنْكَ ذُرِّيَّةً طَيِّبَةً** corresponds to his saying in Surah Maryam: **فَهَبْ لِي مِنْ لَدُنْكَ وَلِيًّا يَرِثُنِي وَيَرِثُ مِنْ آلِ يَعْقُوبَ وَاجْعَلْهُ رَبِّ رَضِيًّا**. His saying there, "good" (طَيِّبَةً), corresponds to his saying here, "and make him, my Lord, pleasing." What is meant by it is what he witnessed of Mary's proximity and nobility before Allah and her righteous deeds. Thus, his saying there, "Grant me from Yourself an offspring," remains corresponding to his saying here, "so grant me from Yourself an heir who may inherit from me and inherit from the House of Jacob," which explains it. Therefore, what is meant by his saying: "an heir who may inherit from me..." is a biological child who inherits from him.

From this, the falsity of what has been said becomes clear: that he (peace be upon him) requested by his saying: "so grant me from Yourself an heir who may inherit from me..." someone to take his place and inherit from him, whether a child or otherwise. Similarly, the falsity of the claim that he despaired of having a child from his wife and thus requested someone from among other people to inherit from him and take his place.

This is because of the explicitness of his saying in the same story in Surah Al 'Imran: **رَبِّ هَبْ لِي مِن لَّدُنْكَ ذُرِّيَّةً طَيِّبَةً** — "My Lord! Grant me a good offspring from Yourself!" — in requesting a child.

Furthermore, the use of an expression like "grant me" (فَهَبْ لِي), which suggests a type of possession, is not appropriate for other people who are strangers; rather, what is suitable for that is the expression of "appointing" (الجعل) and the like, as in the saying of the Exalted: **وَاجْعَلْ لَنا مِنْ لَدُنْكَ وَلِيًّا وَاجْعَلْ لَنا مِنْ لَدُنْكَ نَصِيراً** — "and appoint for us from Yourself a guardian, and appoint for us from Yourself a helper" (4:75).

From this, it also becomes clear that what is meant by his saying: "an heir who may inherit from me" is a child, as he expressed it in the verse of Al 'Imran as "offspring" (الذرية). Thus, what is meant by "heir" (الولي) is the offspring, and he is an heir in inheritance. What is meant by **inheritance (الوراثة)** is the inheritance of what the deceased left behind of wealth and the goods of life. This is what immediately comes to mind from "inheritance" without doubt, either because it is a literal truth (حقيقة) regarding wealth and the like and metaphorical (مجازا) in other things—such as inheritance attributed to knowledge and other spiritual attributes and states—or because it is understood as wealth if it is a literal truth for all. In any case, the word is apparent in the inheritance of wealth, and its conjunction with "heir" (الولي) determines that what is meant by it is a child. This is further clarified by his preceding saying: **وَإِنِّي خِفْتُ الْمَوالِيَ مِنْ وَرائِي** — "Indeed I fear my kinsmen after me," as will be explained later, Allah willing.

As for the opinion of those who say that what is meant by it is the inheritance of prophethood, and that he requested from his Lord to grant him a child who would inherit prophethood from him, it is refuted by what you have just learned: that what drove him (peace be upon him) to this prayer and request was what he witnessed from Mary, and there was no mention of prophethood in that, nor any trace of it. What connection is there between witnessing her worship and nobility and being impressed by it, and requesting from his Lord a child who would inherit prophethood from him?

Moreover, prophethood is not something inherited through lineage, and this is obvious. Even if this were rectified by saying that inheritance means merely the coming of one prophet after another, or the appearance of a prophet from the offspring of a prophet through a type of metaphorical allusion, the problem would arise from another side: its lack of suitability with his subsequent saying: **وَاجْعَلْلهُ رَبِّ رَضِيًّا** — "and make him, my Lord, pleasing." For there is no meaning in one saying: "Grant me a child who is a prophet and make him pleasing." If it were taken as emphasis, it would be emphasizing something with that which is lesser than it. Likewise, the possibility that what is meant by "pleasing" is being pleasing to people is refuted by its contradiction to the absolute use of "well-pleasing," as previously mentioned, along with its lack of suitability to his motive.

Similar in falsity is the opinion of those who say that what is meant by it is the inheritance of knowledge, and that he requested from his Lord to grant him a child who would inherit his knowledge. For there is no meaning in Zechariah witnessing worship and nobility from Mary, being impressed by it, and then requesting from his Lord a child who would inherit his knowledge, without any connection between the motive and the thing requested.

The claim that inheritance means the inheritance of knowledge, and that his saying: "and make him, my Lord, pleasing" means righteous deeds, and that the combination of beneficial knowledge and righteous deeds corresponds to what he witnessed from Mary of sincerity, worship, and nobility—

—is refuted by the fact that his saying: "and make him, my Lord, pleasing" is sufficient on its own to indicate the request for beneficial knowledge and righteous deeds due to its absolute nature. A person who is good in action, regardless of knowledge, is only "well-pleasing in action" and is never called "well-pleasing" (مرضيا) in an absolute sense. Similar to this is the opinion that "pleasing" means being pleasing to people.

Also similar in falsity is the possibility that what is meant by inheritance is the inheritance of godwariness and nobility, and that he requested from his Lord to grant him a child who would inherit his proximity and station before Allah. For it would be more suitable to request a child who possesses what Mary had of proximity and nobility, or absolute proximity and nobility, rather than requesting a child to whom his own proximity and nobility would be transferred.

Furthermore, this does not suit his saying: **وَإِنِّي خِفْتُ الْمَوالِيَ مِنْ وَرائِي** — "Indeed I fear my kinsmen after me." For the apparent context is that he is requesting a child who would inherit from him and to whom would be transferred that which, were it not for him, would be transferred to the kinsmen, and he fears them taking possession of that after his death. There is no meaning in him (peace be upon him) fearing his kinsmen's possession of proximity and station, or their being characterized by godwariness and nobility, neither before his death nor after it. The arena of the prophets is more exalted and pure than such stinginess; they have no wish but the righteousness and felicity of people.

The saying of some that his kinsmen (peace be upon him) were the wicked of the Children of Israel, and thus he feared they would not be good successors to him among his people after him—the response is that if this succession (خلافة) were an internal, divine succession, then it is certainly not inherited through lineage. Moreover, it does not miss its suitable recipient, and none but its worthy people are invested with it; there is no reason to fear that. If it were an external, worldly succession inherited through lineage and the like, then it is a social acquisition and among the goods of the life of this world, similar to wealth. Thus, there is no benefit in diverting the inheritance in the verse from the inheritance of wealth to the inheritance of succession and kingship.

Furthermore, John (peace be upon him) did not assume any of this succession or kingship such that it would be his inheritance which he prevented his father's kinsmen from inheriting. The Children of Israel had no kingdom during the time of Zechariah and John; rather, the Romans were dominant over them and ruling among them.

If you say: "The interpretation of inheritance in the verse as the inheritance of knowledge and the like, rather than wealth, is supported by the fact that in the high perspectives and lofty ambitions of the holy souls—who have detached themselves from the attachments of this vanishing, severed world and connected themselves to the eternal world—there is no inclination toward worldly goods even to the extent of a gnat's wing. This is especially true for Zechariah (peace be upon him), who was famous for his complete detachment and transcendence. It is normally impossible that he would fear the inheritance of wealth and goods which have not the slightest value in his high perspective, or that he would manifest distress, grief, and fear because of it, and make that kind of request from his Lord, which indicates complete love and the heart's attachment to the world and its ornaments."

"And the claim that he feared his kinsmen would spend his wealth after his death in what is not proper, and thus requested from his Lord a well-pleasing heir, is false. For when a man dies and his wealth is transferred through inheritance to another, the wealth becomes the property of the heir; his spending it rightly or wrongly is on his own conscience, and there is no accountability or blame for the deceased in that."

"Moreover, the prevention of this fear was easy for him (peace be upon him) by spending it before his death and giving it all in charity in the way of Allah, leaving his wicked cousins frustrated due to their poor states and ugly deeds. Thus, his intention (peace be upon him) in requesting a child was nothing but the implementation of Allah's laws, the promotion of the Sharia, and the continuation of prophethood in his children."

I say: The objection is based on his saying: **فَهَبْ لِي مِنْ لَدُنْكَ وَلِيًّا يَرِثُنِي** being intended to express the request for the inheritance of wealth for his child, but the reality is otherwise. The primary intention (القصد الأول) of his saying: "an heir who may inherit from me" is nothing but the request for a child, as is also apparent from his saying in Surah Al 'Imran: **هَبْ لِي مِن لَّدُنْكَ ذُرِّيَّةً** — "Grant me a good offspring from Yourself," and his saying in another place: **رَبِّ لا تَذَرْنِي فَرْداً** — "My Lord! Do not leave me without an heir" (21:89).

Rather, his saying: "who may inherit from me" (يَرِثُنِي) is a determining indicator (قرينة معينة) that what is meant by "heir" (الولي) in the discourse is the guardianship of inheritance, which applies to the child. This is because "guardianship" (الولاية) is a general meaning with various instances (مصاديق) that are not determined except by a specific indicator. For example, it was qualified by "help" in His saying: **وَما كانَ لَهُمْ مِنْ أَوْلِياءَ يَنْصُرُونَهُمْ** — "They had no allies to help them" (42:46), where what is meant is the guardianship of help. It was qualified by "commanding and forbidding" in His saying: **وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتُ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِياءُ بَعْضٍ يَأْمُرُونَ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ** — "But the faithful, men and women, are comrades of one another: they bid what is right and forbid what is wrong" (9:71), where what is meant is the guardianship of management. And so on.

Were it not that what is meant by it is the inheritance of wealth and that it serves as a determining indicator, there would remain nothing in the discourse to indicate the request for a child, which is the original objective of the prayer. For the inheritance of knowledge, prophethood, worship, or nobility carries no suggestion that the heir is the child, as admitted by some who interpreted the inheritance in the verse as one of these meanings. Thus, the prayer would remain devoid of any indication of the original objective, and that would be enough to diminish the discourse.

In short, the concern is focused on conveying the request for a child. As for the inheritance of wealth, it is not intended as the primary objective; rather, it is a determining indicator that what is meant by "heir" is the child. Yes, it indicates in itself that if he had a child, he would inherit his wealth. There is nothing in this, nor in his saying: **وَإِنِّي خِفْتُ الْمَوالِيَ مِنْ وَرائِي** —and its state is the same as his saying: "an heir who may inherit from me"—that indicates his heart's attachment (peace be upon him) to the vanishing world or the ornaments of its life, which are but the enjoyment of delusion.

As for the request for a child, it is something upon which Allah has fashioned the human species, whether righteous or wicked, prophet or otherwise. He has equipped everyone with the apparatus of procreation and reproduction and instilled in them what calls them to it. Thus, any one of them, if his nature is not distorted, is driven to request a child and sees the survival of his child after him as his own survival, and their possession of what he possessed of the goods of life—and this is inheritance—as his own possession and the life of his own person.

The divine laws (الشرائع الإلهية) did not nullify this primordial judgment (الحكم الفطري), nor did they disparage this instinctive drive; rather, they praised it and encouraged it. In the Noble Quran, there are many verses indicating this, such as His saying, the Exalted, narrating from Abraham (peace be upon him): **رَبِّ هَبْ لِي مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ** — "My Lord! Give me [an heir] from among the righteous" (37:100), and His saying: **الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي وَهَبَ لِي عَلَى الْكِبَرِ إِسْماعِيلَ وَإِسْحاقَ إِنَّ رَبِّي لَسَمِيعُ الدُّعاءِ** — "All praise belongs to Allah, who, despite [my] old age, gave me Ishmael and Isaac. Indeed my Lord hears all supplications" (14:39), and His saying narrating from the believers: **رَبَّنا هَبْ لَنا مِنْ أَزْواجِنا وَذُرِّيَّاتِنا قُرَّةَ أَعْيُنٍ** — "Our Lord! Give us joy in our wives and offspring" (25:74), among other verses.

If you say: "The aforementioned view regarding the meaning of inheritance was based on what is understood from His saying: **هُنالِكَ دَعا زَكَرِيَّا رَبَّهُ** — 'Thereat Zechariah supplicated his Lord'—that what drove him to request a child was what he witnessed of Mary's worship and her nobility before Allah, the Glorified, so he desired to be granted a child who would resemble her in worship and nobility. However, it is possible that his motive was something else. It has been mentioned in some reports that Zechariah used to find with Mary fruits out of their season—winter fruit in summer and summer fruit in winter. He said to himself: 'If it is not difficult for Allah to provide her with winter fruit in summer and summer fruit in winter, it is not difficult for Him to provide me with a child out of his time, while I am a vanishing old man and my wife is barren.' So he said: **فَهَبْ لِي مِنْ لَدُنْكَ وَلِيًّا يَرِثُنِي**."

"Thus, witnessing the fruit out of its season prompted him to request a child out of his time. But this noble prophet is too exalted to request a child just to inherit his wealth; he only requested him to inherit prophethood, knowledge, or worship and nobility."

I say: There is no evidence from the linguistic context that the provision (الرزق) in His saying: **كُلَّمَا دَخَلَ عَلَيْهَا زَكَرِيَّا الْمِحْرَابَ وَجَدَ عِندَهَا رِزْقًا قَالَ يَا مَرْيَمُ أَنَّى لَكِ هَذَا قَالَتْ هُوَ مِنْ عِندِ اللهِ إِنَّ اللهَ يَرْزُقُ مَن يَشَاءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ** — "Whenever Zechariah visited her in the sanctuary, he would find provisions with her. He said, ‘O Mary, from where does this come for you?’ She said, ‘It is from Allah. Indeed Allah provides for whomever He wishes without any reckoning’" (3:37)—is fruit out of its season, or that what drove Zechariah (peace be upon him) to request a child was witnessing that or Mary's saying: "Indeed Allah provides for whomever He wishes without any reckoning." Had it been so, the indication of it would have been in a more eloquent manner. Rather, the apparent context—especially the beginning of the verse: **فَتَقَبَّلَهَا رَبُّهَا بِقَبُولٍ حَسَنٍ وَأَنبَتَهَا نَبَاتًا حَسَنًا** — "Thereupon her Lord accepted her with a gracious acceptance, and made her grow up in a worthy fashion"—is that the concern is to convey that Mary possessed nobility before her Lord, who provided for her not through ordinary causes. This was the motive for Zechariah (peace be upon him) to request a good offspring and a pleasing child.

Even if that were granted, its implication would be that Zechariah was driven primarily to request offspring and a child. Since he was a noble prophet who had no desire except for a righteous child, he prayed secondly that he be good and pleasing, as indicated by the resumption of the prayer with his saying: **وَاجْعَلْهُ رَبِّ رَضِيًّا** — "and make him, my Lord, pleasing," and the qualification with "good" in his saying: **ذُرِّيَّةً طَيِّبَةً** — "a good offspring."

He expressed this objective of his, as narrated from him in Surah Al 'Imran, with his saying: **هَبْ لِي مِن لَّدُنْكَ ذُرِّيَّةً** — "Grant me a good offspring from Yourself," and in this Surah, after presenting the mention of his old age, his wife's barrenness, and his fear of the kinsmen, with his saying: **فَهَبْ لِي مِنْ لَدُنْكَ وَلِيًّا يَرِثُنِي**. Thus, what is meant by his saying: "an heir who may inherit from me" is the child without doubt, and he was expressed and pointed to by the title of the guardianship of inheritance.

The guardianship of inheritance that is suitable to be a defining title for the child is that which he is specifically characterized by: the guardianship of inheriting the estate. As for the guardianship of inheriting prophethood—if it were permissible to call it a guardianship of inheritance—as well as the guardianship of inheriting knowledge, just as a student inherits the knowledge of his teacher, and the guardianship of inheriting spiritual stations and divine nobilities, these guardianships are foreign to lineage and birth. They may coincide with them or be separate from them. Thus, they are not suitable to be made a definition or a mirror for them except with a strong indicator, and there is nothing in the discourse suitable for that. Everything assumed to be suitable for it is equally suitable for its opposite. In that case, he would have neglected in the prayer what was intended as the primary objective and occupied himself with what lies beyond it, and that would be enough to diminish the discourse.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا زَكَرِيَّا إِنَّا نُبَشِّرُكَ بِغُلامٍ اسْمُهُ يَحْيى لَمْ نَجْعَلْ لَهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ سَمِيًّا** — "‘O Zechariah! Indeed We give you the good news of a son whose name is John. We have not named anyone with this name before’" (19:7). There is a deletion in the discourse for the sake of conciseness (إيجازا), and the estimation is: "So We responded to him and called out to him: O Zechariah, indeed We give you the good news..." and so on. It has been mentioned in Surah al-Anbiya regarding the story: **فَاسْتَجَبْنا لَهُ وَوَهَبْنا لَهُ يَحْيى** — "So We responded to him, and gave him John" (21:90), and in Surah Al 'Imran: **فَنادَتْهُ الْمَلائِكَةُ وَهُوَ قائِمٌ يُصَلِّي فِي الْمِحْرابِ أَنَّ اللهَ يُبَشِّرُكَ بِيَحْيى** — "Then the angels called out to him, as he stood praying in the sanctuary: ‘Allah gives you the good news of John’" (3:39).

The verse of Al 'Imran testifies that His saying: "O Zechariah, indeed We give you the good news..." was a revelation (وحي) through the mediation of the angels. Thus, it is the saying of Allah, the Exalted, which the angels conveyed to Zechariah. This is more apparent in His second saying: **قالَ كَذلِكَ قالَ رَبُّكَ هُوَ عَلَيَّ هَيِّنٌ** — "He said, ‘So shall it be. Your Lord says, ‘‘It is simple for Me...’’’" (19:9).

In the verse is an indication that Allah, the Glorified, is the one who named him John (يحيى), which is His saying: "whose name is John," and that no one had been named with this name before him, which is His saying: **لَمْ نَجْعَلْ لَهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ سَمِيًّا** — "We have not named anyone with this name before," i.e., a partner in the name.

It is not far-fetched that "namesake" (السمي) means "peer" or "like," similar to what will come in His saying, the Exalted: **فَاعْبُدْهُ وَاصْطَبِرْ لِعِبادَتِهِ هَلْ تَعْلَمُ لَهُ سَمِيًّا** — "So worship Him and be steadfast in His worship. Do you know any namesake for Him?" (19:65). This is supported by the fact that Allah, the Glorified, described him in His speech with attributes with which He had not described any of His prophets or allies before him, such as His saying later: **وَآتَيْناهُ الْحُكْمَ صَبِيًّا** — "and We gave him judgement while still a child" (19:12), and His saying: **وَسَيِّداً وَحَصُوراً** — "a master and chaste" (3:39), and His saying: **وَسَلامٌ عَلَيْهِ يَوْمَ وُلِدَ وَيَوْمَ يَمُوتُ وَيَوْمَ يُبْعَثُ حَيًّا** — "Peace be to him the day he was born, and the day he dies, and the day he is raised alive" (19:15). Although the Messiah (peace be upon him) shared these attributes with him—and they were maternal cousins—his birth was after the birth of John (peace be upon him).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ رَبِّ أَنَّى يَكُونُ لِي غُلامٌ وَكانَتِ امْرَأَتِي عاقِراً وَقَدْ بَلَغْتُ مِنَ الْكِبَرِ عِتِيًّا** — "He said, ‘My Lord! How shall I have a son, when my wife is barren, and I have reached extreme old age?’" (19:8). Al-Raghib said: "**The youth (الغلام)** is the one whose mustache has begun to sprout. It is said: a youth (غلام) between youthfulness (الغلومة) and adolescence (الغلومية). The Exalted said: 'How shall I have a son (غلام)?'" He said: "**The youth became lustful (اغتلم)** when he reached the limit of puberty." End quote.

It is said in *al-Majma'*: "**Extreme age (العتي)** and **decrepitude (العسي)** carry the same meaning. It is said: he became extremely old (عتا يعتو عتوا وعتيا) and he became decrepit (عسا يعسو عسوا وعسيا); thus he is extremely old (عات) and decrepit (عاس) when length of time has changed him to a state of dryness and stiffness." End quote. Reaching extreme age is an allusion to the cessation of the desire for marriage and the cutting off of the path to procreation.

His inquiry (peace be upon him) about the existence of the son despite his wife's barrenness and his reaching extreme age—while he had mentioned both matters within his prayer when he said: "My Lord! Indeed my bones have become feeble..." and so on—is based on wonder at the glad tidings and an inquiry into its specifics, rather than on deeming it far-fetched or denial. For one who is given glad tidings of what he does not expect due to the abundance of obstacles and the absence of causes, his soul becomes agitated upon first hearing it. He then begins to ask about the specifics of what he was promised so that his heart may be at ease and the agitation of his soul may subside. He is, nonetheless, certain of the truth of what he was promised, for psychological thoughts may not cease despite the presence of knowledge and faith. Its parallel has already been mentioned in the commentary of His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذْ قالَ إِبْراهِيمُ رَبِّ أَرِنِي كَيْفَ تُحْيِ الْمَوْتى قالَ أَوَلَمْ تُؤْمِنْ قالَ بَلى وَلكِنْ لِيَطْمَئِنَّ قَلْبِي** — "And when Abraham said, ‘My Lord! Show me how You revive the dead,’ He said, ‘Do you not believe?’ He said, ‘Yes indeed, but in order that my heart may be at rest’" (2:260).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ كَذلِكَ قالَ رَبُّكَ هُوَ عَلَيَّ هَيِّنٌ وَقَدْ خَلَقْتُكَ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَلَمْ تَكُ شَيْئاً** — "He said, ‘So shall it be. Your Lord says, ‘‘It is simple for Me, and certainly I created you before when you were nothing’’’’" (19:9). This is a response to what he inquired and asked about, so that his soul might be comforted and his heart calmed. The pronoun in "He said" (قالَ) refers to Him, the Exalted. His saying: "So shall it be" (كَذلِكَ) is the spoken statement (مقول القول) and is the predicate of a suppressed subject; the estimation is: "It is so," meaning the matter will occur as We have informed you in the glad tidings, without doubt.

His saying: "Your Lord says, 'It is simple for Me'" is a second spoken statement for the first "He said." It is in the position of a justification (التعليل) for His saying: "So shall it be," by which any wonder is removed. Nothing fails to occur according to His will; His command, when He intends a thing, is only that He says to it, "Be." Thus, creating a son from a man who has reached extreme old age and a barren woman is simple and easy for Him.

This inquiry and response have been expressed in the narration of the story in Surah Al 'Imran with His saying: **قالَ رَبِّ أَنَّى يَكُونُ لِي غُلامٌ وَقَدْ بَلَغَنِيَ الْكِبَرُ وَامْرَأَتِي عاقِرٌ قالَ كَذلِكَ اللهُ يَفْعَلُ ما يَشاءُ** — "He said, ‘My Lord! How shall I have a son, when old age has already overtaken me and my wife is barren?’ He said, ‘So does Allah do whatever He wishes’" (3:40). Thus, His saying: "Your Lord says, 'It is simple for Me'" here corresponds to His saying there: "Allah does whatever He wishes," which supports the meaning we have presented. His saying here: "and certainly I created you before when you were nothing" is an exposition of some of the instances of creation by which wonder is removed.

In the verse are other views mentioned by commentators: among them is that His saying: "So shall it be" (كَذلِكَ) relates to the second "says" (قالَ), and the entire sentence is the response, meaning: "Your Lord has commanded that and decreed so." His saying: "It is simple for Me" is another spoken statement of the saying, or it was brought by way of narration.

Among them is that the address in His saying: "Your Lord says" is to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and not to Zechariah (peace be upon him). These are views that the context does not support.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ رَبِّ اجْعَلْ لِي آيَةً قالَ آيَتُكَ أَلَّا تُكَلِّمَ النَّاسَ ثَلاثَ لَيالٍ سَوِيًّا** — "He said, ‘My Lord! Appoint a sign for me.’ He said, ‘Your sign is that you shall not speak to people for three nights, [while being] in sound health’" (19:10). It has already been mentioned in the story in Surah Al 'Imran that the delivery of the glad tidings to Zechariah was through the mediation of the angels: "Then the angels called out to him, as he stood praying in the sanctuary: ‘Allah gives you the good news of John’" (3:39). He (peace be upon him) only asked for a sign (الآية) to distinguish truth from falsehood, so that it would indicate to him that what he heard of the call was an angelic revelation and not a satanic suggestion. Therefore, he was answered with a divine sign to which Satan has no access, which was that his tongue would not be released for three days except for the remembrance of Allah, the Glorified; for the prophets are protected by a divine protection (عصمة) such that Satan cannot manipulate their souls.

Thus, his saying: "My Lord! Appoint a sign for me" is a request for a distinguishing sign. His saying: "He said, ‘Your sign is that you shall not speak to people for three nights, [while being] in sound health’" is the response to what he asked. It is that his tongue would be restrained for three days from everything except the remembrance of Allah, while he was "sound" (سوي), i.e., healthy and free from disease or defect.

What is meant by "not speaking to people" is the inability to speak to them, by way of mentioning the necessary consequence (اللازم) and intending the thing necessitated (الملزوم) as an allusion. What is meant by "three nights" is three nights with their days, which is common in usage. Thus, he (peace be upon him) would remember Allah with various types of remembrance but would not be able to speak to people except through gestures and signs. The evidence for all of this is His saying, the Exalted, in the story in Surah Al 'Imran: **قالَ رَبِّ اجْعَلْ لِي آيَةً قالَ آيَتُكَ أَلَّا تُكَلِّمَ النَّاسَ ثَلاثَةَ أَيَّامٍ إِلَّا رَمْزاً وَاذْكُرْ رَبَّكَ كَثِيراً وَسَبِّحْ بِالْعَشِيِّ وَالْإِبْكارِ** — "He said, ‘My Lord! Appoint a sign for me.’ He said, ‘Your sign is that you shall not speak to people for three days except by gestures. And remember your Lord much, and glorify Him morning and evening’" (3:41).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَخَرَجَ عَلى قَوْمِهِ مِنَ الْمِحْرابِ فَأَوْحى إِلَيْهِمْ أَنْ سَبِّحُوا بُكْرَةً وَعَشِيًّا** — "So he emerged to his people from the sanctuary, and signaled to them to glorify [Allah] morning and evening" (19:11). It is said in *al-Majma'*: "**The sanctuary (المحراب)** was named so because the one facing it in his prayer is like one warring (محارب) against Satan over his prayer. Its origin is the assembly of the nobles, before which one fights to defend its people." He said: "**Signaling/Revelation (الإيحاء)** is the casting of a meaning into the soul secretly and quickly. Its origin is from their saying: 'The revelation! The revelation!' (الوحي الوحي), meaning: 'Haste! Haste!'" End quote. The meaning of the verse is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا يَحْيى خُذِ الْكِتابَ بِقُوَّةٍ** — "‘O John! Hold on to the Book with [all your] strength’" (19:12). The mention of holding on to the Book with strength and the command for it has been repeated in His speech, the Exalted, such as His saying: **فَخُذْها بِقُوَّةٍ وَأْمُرْ قَوْمَكَ يَأْخُذُوا بِأَحْسَنِها** — "‘So hold on to them with [all your] strength, and bid your people to hold on to the best of them’" (7:145), and His saying: **خُذُوا ما آتَيْناكُمْ بِقُوَّةٍ وَاذْكُرُوا ما فِيهِ** — "‘Hold on with [all your] strength to what We have given you, and remember that which is in it’" (2:63), and His saying: **خُذُوا ما آتَيْناكُمْ بِقُوَّةٍ وَاسْمَعُوا** — "‘Hold on with [all your] strength to what We have given you, and listen’" (2:93), among other verses. What first comes to mind from their context is that what is meant by holding on to the Book with strength is to realize the knowledge (المعارف) within it and to act upon the laws (الأحكام) within it with care and concern.

In the discourse is a deletion and conciseness for the sake of brevity. The estimation is: "When We gave him John, We said to him: O John, hold on to the Book with strength in the aspects of both knowledge and action." With this meaning, it is supported that what is meant by "the Book" is the Torah, or it and the other books of the prophets; for the Book that contained the Sharia at that time was the Torah.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَآتَيْناهُ الْحُكْمَ صَبِيًّا وَحَناناً مِنْ لَدُنَّا وَزَكاةً** — "and We gave him judgement while still a child, and a compassion from Us and purity" (19:12-13). **Judgement (الحكم)** has been interpreted as understanding, intellect, wisdom, knowledge of the etiquettes of service, truthful intuition, and prophethood. However, what is understood from verses such as His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ الْكِتابَ وَالْحُكْمَ وَالنُّبُوَّةَ** — "Certainly We gave the Children of Israel the Book, judgement and prophethood" (45:16), and His saying: **أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ آتَيْناهُمُ الْكِتابَ وَالْحُكْمَ وَالنُّبُوَّةَ** — "They are the ones whom We gave the Book, judgement and prophethood" (6:89), and other verses, is that judgement is other than prophethood. Thus, interpreting judgement as prophethood is not appropriate. Likewise, its interpretation as knowledge of the etiquettes of service, truthful intuition, or intellect, as there is no evidence from the perspective of the word or the meaning for any of that.

Yes, one might find support from verses such as His saying: **يَتْلُوا عَلَيْهِمْ آياتِكَ وَيُعَلِّمُهُمُ الْكِتابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ وَيُزَكِّيهِمْ** — "who shall recite to them Your signs and teach them the Book and wisdom and purify them" (2:129), and His saying: **يَتْلُوا عَلَيْهِمْ آياتِهِ وَيُزَكِّيهِمْ وَيُعَلِّمُهُمُ الْكِتابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ** — "who recites to them His signs and purifies them, and teaches them the Book and wisdom" (62:2)—and **wisdom (الحكمة)** is a form of judgement—that what is meant by judgement is the knowledge of true divine realities and the uncovering of what lies beneath the veils of the Unseen (الغيب) relative to ordinary perspectives. Perhaps to this returns the interpretation of judgement as understanding. Accordingly, the meaning would be: "We gave him the knowledge of true realities while he was a child who had not yet reached puberty."

His saying: **وَحَناناً مِنْ لَدُنَّا** — "and a compassion from Us" is conjoined to "judgement," meaning: "and We gave him a compassion from Us." **Compassion (الحنان)** is affection and pity. Al-Raghib said: "Because pity is inseparable from mercy, mercy was expressed as compassion in His saying, the Exalted: 'and a compassion from Us.' From it is said: 'The Compassionate, the All-Bounteous' (الحنان المنان) and 'compassion after compassion' (حنانيك)."

Compassion in the verse has been interpreted as mercy (الرحمة). Perhaps what is meant by it is prophethood or guardianship, as in the saying of Noah (peace be upon him): **وَآتانِي رَحْمَةً مِنْ عِنْدِهِ** — "and He has granted me a mercy from Himself" (10:28), and the saying of Salih: **وَآتانِي مِنْهُ رَحْمَةً** — "and He has granted me a mercy from Himself" (11:63).

It has been interpreted as love (المحبة). Perhaps what is meant by it is the love of people for him, similar to His saying: **وَأَلْقَيْتُ عَلَيْكَ مَحَبَّةً مِنِّي** — "and I cast upon you a love from Me" (20:39), meaning no one saw him but that they loved him.

It has been interpreted as his affection for people and his mercy and tenderness toward them; thus he was kind to them, sincere toward them, guiding them to Allah and commanding them to repent. For this reason, he was called in the New Testament "John the Baptist."

It has been interpreted as Allah's compassion toward him; whenever he called his Lord, Allah, the Glorified, responded to him, as mentioned in the report. This indicates that Allah, the Glorified, had a specific compassion for him, as suggested by the indefiniteness (تنكير) of the word.

What the context provides—especially considering the qualification of compassion with His saying: "from Us" (مِنْ لَدُنَّا), and the word is only used for that in which there is no course for ordinary natural causes, or where they are not considered—is that what is meant by it is a specific type of divine affection and attraction between him and his Lord that is unfamiliar. By this, the second and third interpretations fall away. Then He followed it with His saying: **زَكاةً** — "and purity." The root of its meaning is righteous growth (النمو الصالح), and it does not suit the first meaning very well. Thus, what is meant by it is either a compassion from Allah, the Glorified, toward him by taking charge of his affair and caring for his state, upon which he grows; or a compassion and attraction from him toward his Lord, upon which he grew. The growth is the growth of the spirit.

From here, the weakness of what has been said becomes clear: that what is meant by purity (الزكاة) is blessing (البركة), meaning his being blessed, beneficial, and a teacher of good; and what has been said that what is meant by it is charity (الصدقة), and the meaning is: "We gave him judgement while he was a charity that We bestowed upon people," or the meaning is that he is a charity from Allah upon his parents, or the meaning is that the judgement given is a charity from Allah upon him; and what has been said that what is meant by purity is purification from sins.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَكانَ تَقِيًّا وَبَرًّا بِوالِدَيْهِ وَلَمْ يَكُنْ جَبَّاراً عَصِيًّا** — "and he was God-conscious, and good to his parents, and he was not self-willed or rebellious" (19:13-14). **God-conscious (التقي)** is an intensive adjective (صفة مشبهة) from godwariness (التقوى), which is piety regarding the prohibitions of Allah and avoiding the commission of forbidden acts that lead to the punishment of Allah. **Good (البر)** with a *fatha* on the *ba* is an intensive adjective from goodness (البر) with a *kasra* on the *ba*, which is benevolence. **Self-willed (الجبار)**—it is said in *al-Majma'*: "The one who does not see anyone as having a right over him, and in him is tyranny and haughtiness. *Jabbar* among palm trees is that which exceeds the reach of the hand." End quote. Its meaning boils down to being the arrogant, overbearing one who imposes upon people what he wants and does not endure anything from them. This is supported by its being followed by "rebellious" (عَصِيًّا), which is an intensive adjective from disobedience (العصيان), the root of whose meaning is refusal.

From here, it becomes clear that the three sentences are intended to express the sum of his states regarding the Creator and the creation. His saying: "and he was God-conscious" is his state regarding his Lord. His saying: "and good to his parents" is his state regarding his parents. His saying: "and he was not self-willed or rebellious" is his state regarding the rest of people; for he was kind and merciful to them, sincere and humble toward them, helping their weak and guiding the seekers of guidance among them. By this, it also becomes clear that the interpretation by some of His saying: "rebellious" (عَصِيًّا) as meaning "disobedient to his Lord" is not appropriate.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَسَلامٌ عَلَيْهِ يَوْمَ وُلِدَ وَيَوْمَ يَمُوتُ وَيَوْمَ يُبْعَثُ حَيًّا** — "Peace be to him the day he was born, and the day he dies, and the day he is raised alive" (19:15). **Peace (السلام)** is close in meaning to security (الأمن). What appears from the instances of their usage regarding the difference between them is that security is the absence from a place of what a person dislikes and fears, while peace is the place being such that everything a person encounters in it is suitable for him, without him disliking it or fearing it.

The indefiniteness of "peace" is to convey magnification (التفخيم), i.e., a magnificent peace be upon him from what he dislikes in these three days, each of which is the opening of one of the worlds that a person enters and lives in. So peace be upon him the day he was born, such that no dislike touches him in the world that would interfere with his felicity; and peace be upon him the day he dies, for he will live in the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — *the state between death and resurrection* — a blissful life; and peace be upon him the day he is raised alive, for he will live in it with the reality of life, and there shall be no fatigue or exhaustion.

It has been said that the qualification of the resurrection with His saying: "alive" (حَيًّا) is to indicate that he would be killed as a martyr, due to His saying, the Exalted, regarding the martyrs: **بَلْ أَحْياءٌ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ يُرْزَقُونَ** — "rather they are alive with their Lord, provided for" (3:169).

The variation of the expression in His saying: "was born" (وُلِدَ), "dies" (يَمُوتُ), and "is raised" (يُبْعَثُ) is to represent that the salutation is during the state of his life (peace be upon him).

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma'*: It is narrated from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) that he said in his prayer: "I ask You, O *K-H-Y-'A-S* (يا كهيعص)."

In *al-Ma'ani*, with its chain of transmission from Sufyan ibn Sa'id al-Thawri from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) in a tradition, he said: "**كهيعص** means: I am the Sufficient (الكافي), the Guide (الهادي), the Guardian (الولي), the Knower (العالم), the Truthful in promise (الصادق الوعد)."

**I say:** He also narrated in it something similar from Muhammad ibn 'Umarah from him (peace be upon him). It is narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn 'Abbas regarding His saying: **كهيعص**, he said: "Great (كبير), Guide (هاد), Trustworthy (أمين), Mighty (عزيز), Truthful (صادق)." In another wording: "Sufficient" (كاف) instead of "Great." It is also narrated from him through other paths: "Generous (كريم), Guide (هاد), Wise (حكيم), Knower (عليم), Truthful (صادق)." This was also narrated from Ibn Mas'ud and others. The sum of the narrations—as you see—is that the disjointed letters are taken from the beginnings of the Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى) despite their variation, such as the *Kaf* from the Sufficient (الكافي), the Great (الكبير), or the Generous (الكريم), and so on for the others. However, it is not completed for the *Ya*, for it was taken in the narrations from the Guardian (الولي), the Wise (الحكيم), or the Mighty (العزيز) as in some of them. It is narrated in it from Umm Hani from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that its meaning is: "Sufficient (كاف), Guide (هاد), Knower (عالم), Truthful (صادق)," and the letter *Ya* was omitted in the tradition. Some indication of this has already been mentioned in the exposition of the verse.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَمْ أَكُنْ بِدُعائِكَ رَبِّ شَقِيًّا**, he says: "My prayer was not frustrated with You."

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying: **وَإِنِّي خِفْتُ الْمَوالِيَ**, it was said: "They are the paternal uncles and cousins," narrated from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him). Ali ibn al-Husayn and Muhammad ibn Ali al-Baqir (peace be upon them) recited: **وَإِنِّي خِفْتُ الْمَوالِيَ** with a *fatha* on the *kha*, a *shadda* on the *fa*, and a *kasra* on the *ta* [meaning "my kinsmen were light/few"].

**I say:** This was the recitation of a group of the Companions and the Successors.

In *al-Ihtijaj*, Abdullah ibn al-Hasan narrated with his chain of transmission from his fathers (peace be upon them): "When Abu Bakr resolved to prevent Fatima from [possessing] Fadak—and that reached her—she came to him and said to him: 'O son of Abi Quhafah! Is it in the Book of Allah that you inherit from your father and I do not inherit from my father? You have certainly brought a monstrous thing! Have you intentionally abandoned the Book of Allah and cast it behind your backs? For He says in what He narrated of the account of John son of Zechariah: **فَهَبْ لِي مِنْ لَدُنْكَ وَلِيًّا يَرِثُنِي وَيَرِثُ مِنْ آلِ يَعْقُوبَ**...'" the tradition.

**I say:** The content of the narration is reported through paths from the Shia and others. Her argument (peace be upon her) is based on the fact that what is meant by inheritance in the verse is the inheritance of wealth. The discussion on this has already been presented in the exposition of the verse. Some indications of this have also been reported through the paths of the Sunnis. In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, from several authors of the *Jawami'*, from al-Hasan, that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "May Allah have mercy on my brother Zechariah; he had no heirs. And may Allah have mercy on my brother Lot; he used to seek refuge in a strong pillar." It is also narrated in it from al-Faryabi from Ibn 'Abbas, who said: "Zechariah had no children born to him, so he asked his Lord and said: **رَبِّ هَبْ لِي مِنْ لَدُنْكَ وَلِيًّا يَرِثُنِي وَيَرِثُ مِنْ آلِ يَعْقُوبَ**. He said: 'He inherits my wealth from me and inherits prophethood from the House of Jacob.'"

It is said in *Ruh al-Ma'ani*: "The position of the Sunnis is that the prophets (peace be upon them) do not inherit wealth, nor are they inherited from, due to what is authentic according to them of reports. This has also come through the path of the Shia; al-Kulayni narrated in *al-Kafi* from Abu al-Bakhtari from Abu 'Abdillah Ja'far al-Sadiq (may Allah be pleased with him) that he said: 'Indeed the scholars are the heirs of the prophets. That is because the prophets did not leave behind a dirham or a dinar as inheritance; rather, they left behind traditions from their traditions. So whoever takes anything from them has taken an abundant share.' And the word 'only' (إنما) is certainly useful for restriction (الحصر) by the admission of the Shia."

"And the inheritance in the verse is interpreted according to what you have heard. We do not concede that it is a linguistic literal truth (حقيقة لغوية) regarding the inheritance of wealth; rather, it is a literal truth regarding what encompasses the inheritance of knowledge, position, and wealth. It only became specific to wealth due to the prevalence of usage in the convention of the jurists, like the conventional transferred meanings (المنقولات العرفية)."

Even if we were to concede that it is a metaphor (مجاز) in that regard, it is a well-known and common metaphor—especially in the usage of the Majestic Quran—to the extent that it is equivalent to the literal meaning (الحقيقة). Examples of this include His saying, the Exalted: **ثُمَّ أَوْرَثْنَا الْكِتابَ الَّذِينَ اصْطَفَيْنا مِنْ عِبادِنا** "Then We caused those whom We chose of Our servants to inherit the Book" (35:32); and His saying, the Exalted: **فَخَلَفَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ خَلْفٌ وَرِثُوا الْكِتابَ** "Then they were succeeded by a generation who inherited the Book" (7:169); and His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ أُورِثُوا الْكِتابَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ** "Indeed those who were made to inherit the Book after them" (42:14); and His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الْأَرْضَ لِلَّهِ يُورِثُها مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ** "Indeed the earth belongs to Allah, and He lets whomever He wishes of His servants inherit it" (7:128); and **وَلِلَّهِ مِيراثُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** "To Allah belongs the heritage of the heavens and the earth" (3:180).

As for their saying: "There is no reason to deviate from the literal meaning," we say: The reason is indeed established, which is to safeguard the speech of the Infallible (المعصوم) from falsehood; and without its interpretation (تأويل), it would be like "stripping the bark off a thorny tree" (خرط القتاد) — *an Arabic idiom denoting an impossible or extremely difficult task*. Furthermore, the reports indicating that they inherit wealth are not relied upon by critical scholars (النقاد).

Some have claimed that it is not permissible to interpret inheritance here as the inheritance of prophethood (النبوة), lest His saying: **وَاجْعَلْهُ رَبِّ رَضِيًّا** "and make him, my Lord, pleasing [to You]" (19:6) become redundant. We have already presented that which clarifies the flaw in this view. The claim that the acquired nature (كسبية) of a thing prevents it from being inherited is of no consequence, for inheritance has been linked to that which is not acquired in the speech of al-Sadiq (peace be upon him).

Among such instances is also what al-Kulayni narrated in *al-Kafi* from Abu ‘Abdillah (رضي الله عنه), who said: "Indeed, Solomon inherited David, and Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) inherited Solomon (عليه‌السلام)." For the inheritance of the Prophet Solomon cannot be conceived as being anything other than the inheritance of knowledge (العلم), prophethood (النبوة), and the like. [End of quote].

This discussion has a theological (كلامية) dimension pertaining to the matter of Fadak, which was one of the villages of Khaybar and was in the possession of Fatima, the daughter of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The first Caliph seized it from her hand, relying on a hadith he narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): "We prophets do not leave inheritance of wealth; what we leave behind is charity (صدقة)." The dispute over this has been long-standing between the theologians of the Shi‘a and the Ahl al-Sunnah, and it is a type of discussion that lies outside the purpose of this book, so we shall not address it. However, there is an exegetical (تفسيرية) dimension that concerns us because of its connection to the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنِّي خِفْتُ الْمَوالِيَ مِنْ وَرائِي وَكانَتِ امْرَأَتِي عاقِراً فَهَبْ لِي مِنْ لَدُنْكَ وَلِيًّا يَرِثُنِي وَيَرِثُ مِنْ آلِ يَعْقُوبَ وَاجْعَلْهُ رَبِّ رَضِيًّا** "Indeed I fear my kinsmen after me, and my wife is barren. So grant me from Yourself an heir who may inherit from me and inherit from the House of Jacob, and make him, my Lord, pleasing [to You]" (19:5-6).

As for his saying: "This has also been narrated through Shi‘i channels, etc.," the narrations on this matter are not limited to what he quoted from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him); rather, the same meaning has also been narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) through their channels. Its meaning—as it first occurs to the mind of every listener—is that it is not the concern of prophets to be preoccupied with amassing wealth and leaving it to the heirs who remain after them; rather, their concern is to leave wisdom (الحكمة) to those who follow them. This is a sound meaning and a common usage that cannot be rejected.

As for his saying: "We do not concede that it is a linguistic literalism (حقيقة لغوية) for the inheritance of wealth..." until the end of what he mentioned, the point is not whether it is a linguistic literalism for something, or a well-known metaphor, or otherwise, nor is there any insistence on such points. Rather, the point is that inheritance—whether it is a literalism for the inheritance of wealth and a metaphor for things like knowledge and wisdom, or a shared literalism (حقيقة مشتركة) between what pertains to wealth and what pertains to things like knowledge and wisdom—requires a diverting or specifying context (قرينة) to intend the inheritance of knowledge and wisdom. The context of the verse, the other verses of the story in the Surahs of Al ‘Imran and al-Anbiya’, and the surrounding indicators (القرائن الحافة) refuse the intention of inheriting knowledge and the like from the word "inherit me" (يرثني), let alone being diverted toward it or specified by it, as we have previously clarified in the commentary (بيان) on the verse.

Indeed, it is not correct to link inheritance to prophethood (النبوة) according to what is derived from the teaching of the Quran—that it is a divine gift (موهبة إلهية) that does not accept transfer (انتقال) or transformation (تحول). There is no doubt that "leaving behind" (الترك) and "transfer" (الانتقال) are part of the concept of inheritance, such as the inheritance of wealth, property, position, knowledge, and the like. For this reason, the usage of inheritance has not appeared in relation to prophethood (النبوة) or messengership (الرسالة) in the Book or the Sunnah.

As for his saying: "We say the reason is indeed established, which is to safeguard the speech of the Infallible from falsehood," this contains an admission that there is no context (قرينة) to intend other than wealth from the word "inherit me" (يرثني) from the perspective of the context of the verses; rather, the matter is the opposite. They were only forced into the aforementioned interpretation to preserve the outward meaning of the hadith due to its authenticity in their view. The response to this is that there is no meaning for the speech of the Exalted to depend, in its functional indication (الدلالة الاستعمالية), on a separate and specific context from outside the speech of the Exalted, especially when the speech is surrounded by contrary indicators. This is not like the specification (تخصيص) of the narrations of rulings and their qualification (تقييد) of the generalities and absolutes of the verses of rulings; for that is an intervention in the final intended meaning (محصل المراد) of the discourse, not in the indication of the word according to its usage.

Furthermore, there is no basis for the authority (حجية) of solitary reports (أخبار الآحاد) in matters other than legal rulings (الأحكام الشرعية) in which legislative enactment (الجعل التشريعي) is confined, especially when they contradict the Book. All of these are matters explained in the science of the principles of jurisprudence (علم الأصول).

As for his saying: "We have already presented that which clarifies the flaw in this view," he refers to taking His saying: **وَاجْعَلْهُ رَبِّ رَضِيًّا** "and make him, my Lord, pleasing [to You]" as an emphasis for His saying: **وَلِيًّا يَرِثُنِي** "an heir who may inherit from me"—meaning in prophethood—or taking the word "pleasing" (رَضِيًّا) to mean "pleasing to the people" to avoid redundancy in the speech. We have already presented in the commentary on the verse that which clarifies the flaw in this.

In *Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi*, from Abu Basir, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "When Zechariah prayed to his Lord to grant him a male child—and the angels called out to him with what they called—he wished to know if that voice was from Allah. It was revealed to him that the sign of that would be that he would withhold his tongue from speaking for three days." He said: "When he withheld his tongue and did not speak, he knew that none but Allah is capable of that." [The hadith].

In *Tafsir al-Nu‘mani*, with its chain of narration from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), he said: "Amir al-Mu'minin (peace be upon him) said when they asked him about the meaning of revelation (الوحي): 'Among it is the revelation of prophethood (وحي النبوة), the revelation of inspiration (وحي الإلهام), and the revelation of sign (وحي الإشارة).'" He continued until he said: "As for the revelation of sign, it is the saying of the Mighty and Majestic: **فَخَرَجَ عَلى قَوْمِهِ مِنَ الْمِحْرابِ فَأَوْحى إِلَيْهِمْ أَنْ سَبِّحُوا بُكْرَةً وَعَشِيًّا** 'So he emerged before his people from the sanctuary, and signaled to them to glorify [Allah] morning and evening' (19:11)—meaning he signaled to them, as in His saying, the Exalted: **أَلَّا تُكَلِّمَ النَّاسَ ثَلاثَةَ أَيَّامٍ إِلَّا رَمْزاً** 'that you shall not speak to the people for three days except by gestures' (3:41)."

In *al-Majma‘*: From Ma‘mar, who said: "The children said to John: 'Come, let us play.' He said: 'We were not created for play.' So Allah, the Exalted, revealed: **وَآتَيْناهُ الْحُكْمَ صَبِيًّا** 'and We gave him judgement while he was still a child' (19:12)." This has also been narrated from Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (عليه السلام).

**I say:** This meaning was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn ‘Asakir from Mu‘adh ibn Jabal in a raised (مرفوعاً) — *attributed directly to the Prophet* — report, and a similar meaning was narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from ‘Ali ibn Asbat, who said: "I saw Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) as he came out to me, so I looked at him intently—looking at his head and his feet—so that I might describe his stature to our companions in Egypt. While I was doing so, he sat down and said: 'O ‘Ali, indeed Allah has argued regarding the Imamate (الإمامة) with the same argument He used regarding prophethood (النبوة), for He said: **وَآتَيْناهُ الْحُكْمَ صَبِيًّا** "and We gave him judgement while he was still a child" (19:12), and **حَتَّى إِذا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُ وَبَلَغَ أَرْبَعِينَ سَنَةً** "until when he comes of age and reaches forty years" (46:15). Thus, it is possible that wisdom (الحكمة) be given while one is a child, and it is possible that wisdom be given while one is forty years old.'"

**I say:** In the narration, "judgement" (الحكم) is interpreted as "wisdom" (الحكمة), which supports what we have previously mentioned.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, ‘Abd al-Razzaq, Ahmad in *al-Zuhd*, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Qatadah: "Regarding His saying: **وَلَمْ يَكُنْ جَبَّاراً عَصِيًّا** 'and he was not self-willed or disobedient' (19:14), he said: Sa‘id ibn al-Musayyib used to say: The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'There is no one who will meet Allah on the Day of Resurrection except that he has a sin, except for John, son of Zechariah.'" Qatadah said: "And al-Hasan said: The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'John, son of Zechariah, never sinned, nor did he ever desire a woman.'"

In the same source: Ahmad, al-Hakim al-Tirmidhi in *Nawadir al-Usul*, al-Hakim, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "There is no one from the children of Adam except that he has erred or intended a sin, except for John, son of Zechariah—he never intended a sin nor committed one."

**I say:** This meaning is narrated through Sunni channels with various wordings. All of them should be specified (تخصيص) to the people of infallibility (أهل العصمة) among the prophets and Imams, even if they outwardly seem to resist such specification. However, it appears that this arises from the poor expression of the narrators due to their reliance on transmission by meaning (النقل بالمعنى) and their deep immersion in it. In short, the reports regarding the asceticism (زهد) of John (peace be upon him) are too numerous to count. According to them, he (peace be upon him) used to eat herbs and wear palm fibers, and he wept out of fear of Allah until tears carved paths upon his face.

In the same source: Ibn ‘Asakir narrated from Qurrah, who said: "The sky never wept for anyone except for John, son of Zechariah, and al-Husayn ibn ‘Ali; and its redness is its weeping."

**I say:** This meaning was narrated in *al-Majma‘* from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), and at its end: "The killer of John was of illegitimate birth, and the killer of al-Husayn was of illegitimate birth."

In the same source: al-Hakim and Ibn ‘Asakir narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: "Allah revealed to Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): 'I killed seventy thousand for [the blood of] John, son of Zechariah, and I shall kill seventy thousand and seventy thousand for [the blood of] your daughter's son.'"

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Abu Hamzah, from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: "I asked: 'What did He mean by His saying regarding John: **وَحَناناً مِنْ لَدُنَّا وَزَكاةً** "and a compassion from Us and purity" (19:13)?' He said: 'The compassion (تحنن) of Allah.' I asked: 'To what extent did Allah's compassion for him reach?' He said: 'Whenever he said: "O Lord," Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, would say: "At your service (لبيك), O John."'" [The hadith].

In *‘Uyun al-Akhbar*, with its chain of narration to Yasir the servant, who said: "I heard Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him) saying: 'The most desolate (أوحش) this creation can be is in three places: the day he is born and leaves his mother's womb and sees the world; the day he dies and witnesses the Hereafter and its people; and the day he is resurrected and sees rulings he did not see in the abode of the world.'

Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, granted peace (سلم) to John in these three places and secured his fear, saying: **وَسَلامٌ عَلَيْهِ يَوْمَ وُلِدَ وَيَوْمَ يَمُوتُ وَيَوْمَ يُبْعَثُ حَيًّا** 'Peace be upon him the day he was born, and the day he dies, and the day he is raised alive' (19:15). And Jesus, son of Mary, granted peace to himself in these three places, saying: **وَالسَّلامُ عَلَيَّ يَوْمَ وُلِدْتُ وَيَوْمَ أَمُوتُ وَيَوْمَ أُبْعَثُ حَيًّا** 'Peace be upon me the day I was born, and the day I die, and the day I am raised alive' (19:33)."

## (The Story of Zechariah in the Quran)

**His Description (عليه‌السلام):** Allah, the Glorified, described him in His speech with prophethood (النبوة) and revelation (الوحي). He described him at the beginning of Surah Maryam with servitude (العبودية), and mentioned him in Surah al-An‘am among the ranks of the prophets and a number of the righteous (الصالحين), then among the chosen (المجتبين)—who are the sincere (المخلصون)—and the guided (المهديين).

**His Life History:** Nothing of his reports is mentioned in the Quran except his prayer seeking a child, its fulfillment, and his being granted John (peace be upon him). This occurred after he saw what he saw of Mary's affair in her worship and her nobility before Allah.

The Glorified mentioned that Zechariah took guardianship of Mary because she had lost her father, ‘Imran. Then, when she grew up, she secluded herself from people and engaged in worship in a sanctuary (محراب) of hers in the Temple. Zechariah would enter upon her to check on her: **كُلَّما دَخَلَ عَلَيْها زَكَرِيَّا الْمِحْرابَ وَجَدَ عِنْدَها رِزْقاً قالَ يا مَرْيَمُ أَنَّى لَكِ هذا قالَتْ هُوَ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ إِنَّ اللهَ يَرْزُقُ مَنْ يَشاءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسابٍ** "Whenever Zechariah visited her in the sanctuary, he would find provisions with her. He said, ‘O Mary, from where does this come to you?’ She said, ‘It is from Allah. Allah provides for whomever He wishes without any reckoning’" (3:37).

Thereupon, Zechariah prayed to his Lord and asked Him to grant him from his wife a pure progeny, while he was a frail old man and his wife was barren. His prayer was answered, and the angels called out to him while he stood praying in the sanctuary that Allah gives him the good news of a boy named John. He asked his Lord for a sign to reassure his soul that the call was from His side, the Glorified.

It was said to him: "Your sign is that your tongue shall be tied, so you shall not speak to people for three days except by gestures." It was so. He emerged before his people from the sanctuary and signaled to them to glorify [Allah] morning and evening. Allah cured his wife for him, and she bore him John (peace be upon him). (3:37-41; 19:2-11; 21:89-90).

The Quran does not mention the end of his affair (peace be upon him) or the manner of his passing. However, numerous reports have been narrated through both Sunni and Shi‘i channels that his people killed him. This was because his enemies sought to kill him, so he fled from them and took refuge in a tree, which opened for him. He entered its trunk, and then it closed. Satan then pointed him out to them and ordered them to saw the tree with a saw. They did so and cut him in half, and he (peace be upon him) was killed at that moment.

It has been mentioned in some reports that the reason for his killing was that they accused him regarding Mary and her pregnancy with the Messiah, saying: "He alone was the one frequenting her and entering upon her." Other reasons have also been suggested.

## (The Story of John (peace be upon him) in the Quran)
